For example so-called “SIM Swap” attacks, SS7 attacks, Port-out fraud, etc. All of these attacks however do require some level of sophistication, whether it be high level access to SS7, or account information or social engineering to successfully port out the phone number to a new provider or swap the sim on the existing account.

There is however other vulnerabilities that are not particularly well known. For VoIP numbers in particular, which may be assigned to a CLEC or VoIP wholesaler, the SMS may need to be routed to a different carrier than the carrier of record. This is accomplished in two different ways. One is an ALT SPID, which NPAC defines as “The four-digit identifier of a second service provider associated with a telephone number or thousand block. It identifies the wholesale service provider customer to which the PSTN service provider has assigned the number. The second service provider in turn may either assign the number to its retail customer or to another service provider for its use.” ALT SPIDs are vulnerable and susceptible to change and can be used to hijack SMS, but it too does require carrier-level access to make changes directly to NPAC. In particular, and importantly, it requires the current provider’s co-operation for the new carrier’s ALT SPID to be added in NPAC.

Which brings us to an alternative SMS routing provider, NetNumber. NetNumber has a product called NetNumber ID (NNID), it’s a 6 digit number similar to an ALT SPID that identifies the carrier to route to for SMS. Net Number explains it in this 2019 Q&A:

We quickly found that every industry database, in every country around the world, has its own model for identifying a communications service provider. The Local Exchange Routing Guide (LERG) database uses a 4-digit Operating Company Number (OCN), the Number Portability Administration Center (NPAC) portability database uses 10-digit Local Routing Number (LRN), most Home Location Registers (HLRs) utilize a 5 or 6-digit Home Network Identity (HNI), etc.

In order to make these databases useful for global routing, the NetNumber team created a globally unique naming convention called NetNumber ID (NNID) that is now widely used by fixed-line, mobile, cable/ Multiple System Operator (MSO) and Over the Top (OTT) service providers to route calls and messages on a global basis. The NetNumber data service team maintains and constantly updates the assignment of NNIDs to every service provider in the world so that our customers can easily identify a destination operator using our global data sources. NNID has become so popular and widely adopted that it has become common to hear service providers say that they “route on NNID.”

As a result, there are many VoIP providers that offer “off net” “text enablement”. Companies such as ZipWhip that promise to let you “Text enable your existing business phone number.” so that customers can text your main business line whether it be VoIP, toll-free or a landline number. There are a plethora of other wholesale VoIP providers that allow you to become a reseller with little to no verification, many of them allow agreements known as “blanket LOAs” where you as the reseller promise that you have an LOA on file for any number you want to text enable for your resellers or end-users. In essence, once you have a reseller account with these VoIP wholesalers you can change the Net Number ID(NNID) of any phone number to your wholesale provider’s NNID and begin receiving SMS text messages with virtually no authentication whatsoever. No SIM Swap, SS7 attacks, or port outs needed — just type the target’s phone number in a text box and hit submit and within minutes you can start receiving SMS text messages for them. They won’t even be alerted that anything has happened as their voice & data services will continue to work as usual. Surprisingly, despite the fact that I publicly disclosed this in 2018, nothing has been done to stop this relatively unsophisticated attack.

NetNumber does have the ability to ‘lock’ specific numbers/carriers from being hijacked, however this doesn’t really solve the issue. As an unregulated quasi-authority for SMS routing they have made mistakes in the past. For example, this specific issue of number hijacking was highlighted in a QSI Consulting Exploratory Paper when ZipWhip had been using anti-competitive tactics to insert themselves as the sole SMS text provider for toll free numbers. “The arrangements between the mobile providers and Zipwhip created a de facto monopoly provider for Toll-Free texts to and from roughly 100 percent of the nation’s mobile subscribers.” (Page 6, QSI Consulting Exploratory Paper).

In addition to the ability to hijack numbers without authorization, ZipWhip had at the time refused to release numbers for SMS text messaging purposes back to their authorized customers. SOMOS Letter to FCC, WT Docket №08–7 (07/01/2016) (“For example, Zipwhip refused to recognize an authorized change of service providers and blocked legitimate messaging traffic. Only after intervention by CTIA and Somos did Zipwhip finally relent and allow the messaging traffic to flow through to the legitimate and authorized customer.”)

All of this was enabled by NetNumber who would assign ZipWhip’s NetNumber ID(NNID) to these toll free numbers, and who would presumably refuse other SMS providers to assign their own NNID to these toll-free numbers. While this situation was changed only after SOMOS and CTIA intervention, in our testing we found that Bandwidth.com numbers — or numbers that had Bandwith.com’s NNID assigned to them — could similarly not be hijacked, while virtually all other numbers could have their NNID re-assigned or hijacked without any prior authorization whatsoever, including wireless numbers.

Up until sometime on Thursday, March 11th, 2021 NetNumber was allowing any and all wireless phone numbers to have their NNIDs reassigned or hijacked without any authorization or verification as well. Presumably while this author and other journalists were seeking comment, after a proof of concept was demonstrated, it appears they have devised a scheme to pretend this is no longer a problem by temporarily not allowing wireless numbers to be hijacked. Their quick fix however brings more questions than answers. If wireless numbers are locked to the carrier they’re already assigned to then what is the purpose of NetNumber’s database? The carrier information about a wireless number can easily be looked up in NPAC without querying NetNumber to see where to send an SMS message to, and why decide only now to offer this protection to wireless numbers and only wireless numbers? Furthemore, people use VoIP numbers instead of their real wireless numbers for various services and those folks are still left vulnerable to this attack while only those who don’t care about their privacy and use their real mobile numbers are protected. The point here is until NetNumber is regulated by the FCC (and the various other telecom regulatories that NetNumber operates in), nothing in their database can be trusted. They make unilateral policy decisions on the fly for whatever purpose suits them. To paraphrase Orwell’s Animal Farm, some carriers are more equal than others.

To demonstrate this point further the country of Barbados has 2 main mobile providers, Flow & Digicel. As of the date of this article, it is believed it’s still possible to hijack any barbados mobile number on these carriers through NetNumber — we’ve only tested and verified on Flow though. Since most providers “route on NNID” attackers hijacking Barbados mobile numbers would succeed in intercepting SMS from 2FA companies like Twilio that use NetNumber to send SMS. However, locally on the island of Barbados, Flow & Digicel route to each other without any NNID lookup, so the victim’s texts locally would still flow back and forth with no suspicion at all that their text messages internationally with carriers that use NetNumber, and importantly text messages from almost any 2FA service on the internet, were being hijacked. This is likely the case for most if not all of the caribbean islands in NANP’s +1 region.

Enter Okey Monitor, a new service that promises to alert you to this type of attack, as well as other SMS intercept attacks(such as port outs, etc.). Their website states:

The unspoken truth is, out-of-band security gaps exist which allow hackers to virtually steal your phone from anywhere. In today’s world, if they have your phone, they are ‘Authentically’ you. Now they can easily pass Two-Factor Authentication and take over your accounts. Hijack your text messages. Impersonate you. All without you knowing a thing.

The key to protect against Communication Hijack Attacks…

You need to be aware of unauthorized changes to your mobile service while it’s happening, in real-time, then take action. We have the solution.

Sign up for our free beta and we’ll monitor out-of-band communications such as your routes and carrier settings. If a malicious event takes place, we’ll alert you through alternative forms of trusted communication. Now you’ll know.

They are currently offering a free beta monitoring service for new users that monitors your number for NNID reassignment, carrier port outs and other changes. I’m told that they plan on offering other protections as well in the future such as SIM swaps and call forwarding change detection (another attack people use is social engineering your provider to call forward your phone number to there’s and then using the voice option instead of SMS to receive two factor authentication codes). When a change is detected the service alerts you by sending an alert on contingency out-of-band point of contacts, including texts to different contingency phone numbers, email alerts and coming soon they’ll have the ability to send alerts over Signal, telegram, Keybase, and possibly other encrypted messaging platforms when a change in SMS routing is detected.

Full Disclosure: The author is the Director of Information and has a minority stake in Okey Systems, LLC

item.title: Seasonal energy storage in aluminium for 100% solar heat and electricity supply
item.description:
Author links open overlay panel
Under a Creative Commons license
open access

Highlights

•

Aluminium redox cycles are promising candidates for seasonal energy storage.

•

Energy that is stored chemically in Al may reach 23.5 MWh/m3.

•

Power-to-Al can be used for storing solar or other renewable energy in aluminium.

•

Hydrogen and heat can be produced at low temperatures from aluminium and water.

•

≈500 kg Al are needed for a 100% solar PV supplied dwelling in Central Europe.

Abstract

In order to reduce anthropogenic global warming, governments around the world have decided to reduce CO2 emissions from fossil fuels dramatically within the next decades. In moderate and cold climates, large amounts of fossil fuels are used for space heating and domestic hot water production in winter. Although on an annual base solar energy is available in large quantities in these regions, least of the solar resource is available in winter, when most of the energy is needed. Therefore, solutions are needed to store and transfer renewable energy from summer to winter. In this paper, a seasonal energy storage based on the aluminium redox cycle (Al3+ → Al → Al3+) is proposed. For charging, electricity from solar or other renewable sources is used to convert aluminium oxide or aluminium hydroxide to elementary aluminium (Al3+ → Al). In the discharging process, aluminium is oxidized (Al → Al3+), releasing hydrogen, heat, and aluminium hydroxide or aluminium oxide as a by-product. Hydrogen is used in a fuel cell to produce electricity. Heat produced from the aluminium oxidation process and by the fuel cell is used for domestic hot water production and space heating. The chemical reactions and energy balances are presented, and simulation results are shown for a system that covers the entire energy demand for electricity, space heating and domestic hot water of a new multi-family building with rooftop photovoltaic energy in combination with the seasonal Al energy storage cycle. It shows that 7–11 kWp of photovoltaic installations and 350–530 kg Al would be needed per apartment for different Swiss climates. Environmental life cycle data shows that the global warming potential and non-renewable primary energy consumption can be reduced significantly compared to today's common practice of heating with natural gas and using electricity from the ENTSO-E network. The presumptive cost were estimated and indicate a possible cost-competitiveness for this system in the near future.

Abbreviations

cr
crystalline
CED
Cumulated Energy Demand
DHW
Domestic Hot Water
ENTSO-E
European Network of Transmission System Operators for Electricity
ESCO
Energy Service Company
EU
European Union
g
gaseous
GWP
Global Warming Potential (IPCC 100 years)
hp
heat pump
HHV
Higher Heating Value – gross heating value (kWh/kg)
IPCC
Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change
l
liquid
LCA
Life Cycle Assessment
MFH
Multi-Family House (apartment building)
NR
Non-Renewable
PCM
Phase Change Material
PV
Photovoltaic
s
solid
SH
Space Heat
SIA
Schweizerischer Ingenieur- und Architektenverein – Swiss society of engineers and architects
TES
Thermal Energy Storage
US DOE
US Department of Energy

Keywords

Seasonal energy storage
Power-to-X
Aluminium redox cycle

1. Introduction

1.1. Motivation

Heating and cooling for buildings and industry accounts for half of the energy consumption of the European Union (EU), and 84% of this consumption is still generated from fossil fuels [1]. Energy demand of households, including electricity, consumes 25% of the final energy consumption of the EU [2].

The electric energy demand in Europe is fairly constant over the year. Fig. 1 shows that the production of electricity in winter is only 13% higher than in summer [3]. On the other hand, heat demand increases strongly in winter, especially in central and northern European climates, where much less solar energy is available during this season. An example is given in Fig. 2, where the delivery of heat is presented for each month of the year based on data of the district heating of Munich [4]. If solar energy shall become one of the main energy suppliers in the future, seasonal energy storage solutions will be needed especially for covering winter heating demands in these climates. Different materials have been proposed for sensible, latent and thermochemical storage of heat or for converting renewable electricity to an energy vector that can be stored. The first step of Power-to-X technologies is usually the electrolysis of water and the production of hydrogen (X = H2). Due to its low molecular weight and small molecular size, as well as due to safety reasons, hydrogen is not well suited for storing large amounts of energy over long time-periods. Therefore, attempts are made to produce other gases or liquids such as methane or methanol (X = CH4 or X = CH3OH), or to develop materials that can store hydrogen with higher density. In general, each conversion step requires energy, i.e. contributes to conversion losses, and many of the proposed solutions require a carbon source in order to produce a hydrocarbon fuel from H2.

Aluminium can be used to produce hydrogen and heat in reactions that yield 0.11 kg H2 and, depending on the reaction, 4.2–4.3 kWh of heat per kg Al. Thus, the volumetric energy density of Al (23.5 MWh/m3)1 outperforms the energy density of hydrogen or hydrocarbons, including heating oil, by a factor of two (Fig. 3). Aluminium (Al) electrolysis cells can produce elementary Al from aluminium oxide (Power-to-Al). The efficiency of this process is approximately 50%, and it is estimated that it may be increased to almost 65% with non-consumable electrodes, wetted cathodes, lower temperature electrolysis cells, and reduction of heat losses [5]. With this process, a solid material (Al) is obtained directly and can be stored without losses as long as desired, with much less safety concerns than for hydrogen or hydrocarbons.

1.2. Photovoltaic and heat pump systems

With well insulated building shells in combination with photovoltaics (PV) and heat pump (hp) systems, net zero energy buildings can be designed. On an annual base, photovoltaics are providing all the electricity needed for household appliances as well as for the heat pump that supplies space heating and domestic hot water [6], [7], [8]. However, in these systems electricity is exported to the electricity grid in summer, and imported in winter. Thus, the goal of the EU to foster nearly zero energy buildings [9] may not only lead to an increased number of buildings with large areas of PV integrated into their roofs and facades, but also to excess PV production in summer on a local, national or even international scale. Furthermore, the question how to cover the additional electricity demand in winter without falling back on fossil fuels or nuclear power plants needs to be addressed. Power-to-X technologies that transform electric power to an energy vector that can be stored are discussed for this purpose. The most prominent examples in this respect are power-to-gas and derived technologies [10].

Based on today’s market available technologies, a single-family home that is insulated according to common current standards in Europe can achieve net zero energy with a heat pump in combination with a 7.2 kWp PV installation that covers both the electricity demand of the heat pump as well as household electricity [7]. With the addition of thermal storage (900 l combi-tank) and a household battery (7 kWh capacity), such systems have been shown to increase PV self-consumption and self-sufficiency to over 50%, and reduce the grid electricity demand to 20% of the useful energy delivered that is defined as the sum of household electricity and heat for space heating and domestic hot water [11]. However, the last 20% of the energy balance relies on electricity exchange with the grid, and creates a mismatch between the demand (higher in winter) and the supply (higher in summer) of electricity. Therefore, a compact seasonal energy storage is needed to further reduce the demand of non-renewable energy of buildings in winter.

1.3. Seasonal energy storage based on Al

The idea to use aluminium as an energy carrier is not new. Rockets have been propelled by combustion of aluminium [12], and experiments with aluminium combustion for combined heat and power generation were carried out in the 80ies and 90ies in Switzerland [13].

A review article on different ways of using aluminium as an energy carrier and storage has been presented by Shkolnikov et al. [14], and experimental work has been conducted by various authors [15], [16], [17], [18].

The main advantages associated with the use of aluminium as an energy storage are:

•

very high energy storage density, both by weight (8.7 kWh/kg) and even more by volume (23.5 MWh/m3);

•

unproblematic storability and transportability;

•

abundantly available in the earth’s crust (third most frequent element);

•

relatively low cost and well-known material.

As can be seen from Fig. 3, the closest competitors in terms of volumetric energy storage density are coal and hydrocarbons that rely on a carbon source for their production from power-to-X technologies. Drawbacks of these technologies are that carbon sources are not always readily available, and the technical or biological fixation of carbon dioxide from air is increasing cost and reducing the energetic efficiency of these power-to-X chains.

In order to overcome the mismatch between the availability of renewable, in particular solar energy, in summer and the demand of heat and electricity in winter, we are proposing a seasonal energy storage based on the aluminium redox cycle (Al3+ → Al → Al3+). In section 2, the analysis of the components of an energy system that can provide 100% of the heat and electricity demand of a multi-family building all year around by a PV and heat pump system combined with an aluminium redox seasonal energy storage is presented. In Section 3, system simulations for the supply of heat and electricity of a multi-family building are presented, and in Section 4 cost estimations and environmental indicators are compared with a system that covers heat demand with a gas fired device and household energy with electricity from the European ENTSO-E network.

2. Methods and data

2.1. Power-to-Al

Aluminium is currently produced on industrial scale by the Hall-Héroult process. In this smelting process, alumina (Al2O3) is dissolved in an electrolyte that contains molten cryolite (Na3AlF6) at 960 °C under ambient pressure. Most of the energy required for this electrolysis is introduced in form of direct current electricity. However, carbon anodes are also used and they are converted to CO2, contributing to a non-negligible extent both to the energy balance as well as to the global warming potential of the process. The chemical reaction of the Hall-Héroult smelter process can be summarized as follows:

(1)2Al2O3+3C→4Al+3CO2;ΔHr,298=2170kJ/mol

According to several sources [13], [19], [20], [21], [22], the overall energetic consumption of a modern Hall-Héroult process is about 12.4 kWh of electricity plus 0.4 kg carbon electrodes (contributing with 3.8 kWh) per kg of aluminium produced. Considering that the theoretical minimum electricity consumption of this process is 6.23 kWh [23], the overall electric efficiency is around 50%. An electricity consumption of only 11.5 kWh per kg aluminium has been reported recently by Hydro [24].

Alumina is usually obtained from bauxite ore, which is the world's primary source of aluminium, typically found in top soil located in various tropical and subtropical regions. The chemical process for producing 1 kg of alumina from bauxite needs about 8 kWh of primary energy [19], [22]. However, in the aluminium storage concept proposed in this paper, this effort has to be made only once since the aluminium is regenerated within the proposed closed material cycle.

Depending on the chemical reaction that is chosen for conversion of aluminium to heat, the oxidized aluminium product will either be in form of alumina (Al2O3) or aluminium hydroxide (Al(OH)3). In the latter case, alumina will have to be obtained from aluminium hydroxide in a calcination process. As can be seen from Eqs. (2) and (3), the theoretical energy requirement of this step depends to a large extent on the question whether the produced water from the process can be condensed to the liquid state or remains gaseous.

(2)2AlOH3→Al2O3+3H2O(l);ΔHr,298=21kJ/mol

(3)2AlOH3→Al2O3+3H2O(g);ΔHr,298=154kJ/mol

The minimum energy required for this step corresponds to 0.1 kWh per kg Al for Eq. (2) and 0.8 kWh/kg for Eq. (3). According to Springer & Hasanbeigi [22], current practiced processes consume around 0.83 kWh per kg alumina (Al2O3), which corresponds to 1.6 kWh per kg Al.

The overall energy consumption and direct emissions of CO2 from calcination and the subsequent Hall-Héroult process, are shown in Table 1.

Table 1. Energy intensity and direct CO2 emissions for the production of aluminium from aluminium-hydroxide, per kg aluminium, according to today's practice of calcination and the Hall-Héroult process.

ProcesskWhkg CO2Reference
Calcination1.6[22]
Consumption of carbon electrodes3.81.5–1.76[20]
Electricity for smelter process11.5–12.4[13], [20], [21], [22], [24]
Total16.9–17.81.76

In order to reduce the global warming impact of the aluminium reduction, inert anode technology [5] may be used that converts alumina to aluminium and O2 according to Eq. (4):

(4)2Al2O3→4Al+3O2;ΔHr,298=3350kJ/mol

This process takes place at ambient pressure, and, depending on the electrolyte used, at temperatures between 700 and 960 °C [25]. The theoretical minimum of energy required for the conversion of aluminium hydroxide to aluminium is shown in Table 2.

Table 2. Minimum energy intensity for the production of aluminium from aluminium-hydroxide by calcination and inert anode smelter cells, according to thermodynamic limits, per kg aluminium.

ProcessMinimum kWhReferences
Calcination0.1 (0.8a))Eqs. (2) + (3)
Electricity for smelter process with inert electrodes8.6Eq. (4)
Total8.7 (9.4a))
a)

assuming that the heat of vaporization from the water released by the calcination process cannot be recovered.

Three types of inert anodes have been proposed for aluminium smelter cells [20]:

•

ceramics – electrodes made from inorganic, non-metallic, solid materials, e.g. SnO2-Sb2O3-CuO

•

cermet – a mixture of ceramics and metals, e.g. NiFe2O4 + NiO + Cu + Ag

•

metal electrodes, e.g. alloys made of Ni + Fe + Cu

A short summary of the advantages and disadvantages of all three types are given by different authors [5], [25], [26]. The challenge is to find a material that is cost-efficient and corrodes only slowly in the highly aggressive environment of the electrolyte. Corrosion and erosion lead to frequent replacement (and thus cost) of the electrodes on the one hand, and to impurities of the produced aluminium that stem from the corroded material on the other hand. The second problem is highly important for the metallurgic industry that needs to deliver high quality construction Al material. However, it is likely that the purity is not of high relevance if aluminium is to be used as an energy carrier only.

The advantages of inert anodes are obvious, and several companies and joint ventures are working on the development of inert anode aluminium smelters. Examples are Rusal [22], Alcoa [20], the EU funded AGRAL project [27], Aluminum Corporation of China [28], and Arctus Metals [29].

To the best of our knowledge, a solar driven inert electrode aluminium smelter process has not been demonstrated yet.

2.2. Production of hydrogen and heat from aluminium

When the passivating oxide layer is removed from its surface, aluminium reacts exothermic with liquid or gaseous water. This reaction produces hydrogen (H2) and, depending on the reaction temperature, different aluminium compounds [30], [31], [32]. Up to a temperature of 280 °C, aluminium hydroxide Al(OH)3 is the most stable product (Eq. (5)). Between 280 °C and 480 °C, aluminium oxide hydroxide is produced (Eq. (6)), and above 480 °C aluminium oxide, also known as alumina, is produced (Eqs. (7) and (8)).

(5)2Al+6H2O(l)→2AlOH3+3H2;ΔHr,298=-839kJ/mol

(6)2Al+4H2O(l)→2AlOOH+3H2;ΔHr,298=-862kJ/mol

(7)2Al+3H2O(g)→Al2O3+3H2;ΔHr,298=-950kJ/mol

(8)2Al+3H2O(l)→Al2O3+3H2;ΔHr,298=-817kJ/mol

For the calculation of the enthalpy of reaction of Eq. (5), a formation enthalpy of ΔHf, 298 = −1290 kJ/mol has been adopted from literature [30], [33], [34] for Al(OH)3, although data was scarce and differing slightly.

A review of hydrogen production from aluminium was given by Wang et al. [35]. Several methods have been investigated and applied successfully for the activation of passivated aluminium surfaces, e.g.:

•

Using an activator such as strong lyes (alkaline solutions): NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)2 [16], [17], [36], [37];

•

Grinding and milling aluminium just prior to the addition of water or while already mixed with water, possibly adding also salts [38];

•

Increasing the temperature and using water vapour to react with aluminium according to Eq. (7) [39];

•

Using seawater with the addition of NaAlO2 [17];

•

Using ultrasonic procedures for the preparation of the samples [40];

•

Addition of Li-based [18], [41] or choline hydroxide [42] activators.

The conversion of aluminium to hydrogen in alkaline solutions reaches nearly 100% of the theoretical yield according to the stoichiometric reaction [17], [41], which was also confirmed by our own laboratory results [43]. This leads to a first estimation of the idealized energy and material balance as shown in Fig. 4.

2.3. The system and its components

2.3.1. System concept

The overall system concept for heating and electricity production for a building from on-site energy harvesting is shown in Fig. 5. The base is a PV and heat pump system with short term battery and thermal storage that supplies electricity and heat to the building. This system has been completed with the aluminium converter and a fuel cell for the supply of electricity and heat in winter when PV does not cover the whole demand. In summer, the PV system produces more electricity than needed for the operation of the system and the supply of the household. This electricity is transferred through the electricity grid to an energy service provider (ESCO) that runs the Power-to-Al process. This process consists of the calcination step and the aluminium smelter (electrolysis), converting oxidized aluminium into elementary aluminium and thus into a storable solar fuel for use in winter.

After aluminium is produced, its surface is oxidized rapidly when in contact with air, forming a protective oxide layer of 0.3–0.4 nm, that prevents further oxidation of the bulk material [44]. Thus, for a sphere of 1 mm diameter, the loss of chemical energy stored in the Al due to this oxidation process is far less than 1‰ and thus insignificant. Al solar fuel is transported from the centralized industrial site (Power-to-Al process) to the site of use, and oxidized aluminium is transported from to the building back to the industrial site to complete the material cycle.

2.3.2. Fuel cells

According to various sources, the theoretical efficiency (hydrogen to electricity) of fuel cells is around 80%. However, in practical applications, efficiencies between 40 and 60% are realistic. According to Mitzel & Friedrich [45], Mitsubishi Hitachi Power systems has installed a 250 kW demonstration plant with an efficiency of 55% in Japan in 2015, and DLR (Germany) has announced to produce a 30 kW prototype fuel cell with an electric efficiency of around 60% in the coming years. Because of these relatively high efficiencies that can possibly be reached also for small scale installations in the range of few kWel, fuel cells are the preferred option for the conversion of hydrogen into electricity.

2.3.3. Battery

For the envisaged system concept, a short term electric storage will be needed for diurnal storage and to even out short term mismatch between the production of electricity by PV or the fuel cell and the local demand. This can be achieved by any short term storage that is provided by the grid operator, e.g. in form of pumped hydro or district battery storage, or it can be covered by a small home-battery unit. In recent years, Li-ion batteries have become the dominant technology in this field. The cost of these technologies have been reduced dramatically and it is expected to further decrease substantially within the next five to ten years.

2.3.4. Heat pumps

Heat pumps are already the dominant technology for heating new built houses in some countries like Switzerland and Sweden, and are thought of being key elements for the future heat supply of Europe’s building stock. They are integral parts of most nearly zero and net zero energy building concepts [47].

2.3.5. Thermal energy storage

Thermal energy storage (TES) has been shown to be advantageous in PV and heat pump combinations, since they can shift heat pump operation towards times when PV electricity is available [7], [11]. Thus electricity for running the heat pump does not have to be stored in batteries that are more expensive and have a higher environmental impact than a TES.

2.3.6. Control

Controlling the charging and discharging of the storages and the hydrogen production will be essential for the envisaged heat and electricity supply system. Possibly, such a system could not only contribute to a 100% solar heating and electricity coverage of a building, but with overcapacity of the hydrogen converter and the fuel cell it could also contribute to stabilize the grid when there is a high demand for electricity in winter.

2.3.7. Transport logistics

The production of hydrogen from aluminium and the conversion of hydrogen to electricity and heat in a fuel cell may be carried out by small units at temperatures below 100 °C. Thus, these units can be placed within a single or multi-family building without much space requirements. On the other hand, the smelting or electrolysis process that converts aluminium oxide to elementary aluminium is carried out at temperatures between 700 °C and 1000 °C and requires a large size and capacity in order to be efficient. This process needs to be carried out at an industrial site that is connected to the private homes by the electricity grid. Therefore, aluminium fuel has to be transported from the industrial site to the building and place of heat demand, and solid products from the aluminium reaction (e.g. aluminium hydroxide) have to be transported back from the end user to the industrial site. Thus, a service similar to providing heating oil or pellets for heating today is envisaged, with transport volumes and weights that are likely to be slightly lower or comparable to conventional fuels such as heating oil that is used today.

2.4. System simulations

Dynamic simulations using the simulation environment TRNSYS-17 [48] were used to evaluate the proposed system depicted in Fig. 5. A reference multi-family building was defined and climatic conditions from different locations in Switzerland were used for the analyses. The basic components to model the proposed system are: PV, Al-to-H2 converter, fuel cell, battery, heat pump, sensible thermal storage, building, and system control. The time step of the yearly simulations was set to 2 min. As a verification process several systematic checks were done for all simulations.

2.4.1. Weather data

In order to assess the behaviour of the system in different locations, weather data from the Swiss society of engineers and architects SIA was used [49].The SIA weather data uses the standard ISO 15927-4:2005 [50] to generate a collection of so called Design Reference Years. This collection is based on measurement data from the years 1984 to 2003. Four locations have been chosen in order to cover the most relevant climatic regions of Switzerland: Davos (DAV), Geneva (GEN), Zurich (KLO) and Locarno (OTL).

Fig. 6 shows the monthly solar irradiation on the horizontal surface for each of the selected locations. Davos is an alpine location with very cold winters. However, it has a very high irradiation in winter. Locarno is located south of the Alps and is characterized by very mild winters and also high irradiation in winter. Geneva is located in the south-west of Switzerland. It has an overall solar irradiation similar to Locarno, but with significantly different distribution over the year, i.e. less irradiation in winter, but more in summer. The last selected location is Zurich, located in the North-Central part of Switzerland at the lake of Zurich. Zurich has significantly less yearly irradiation compared to the other locations.

2.4.2. Simulated building loads

A multi-family house (MFH), as described by Mojic et al. [51], was used as reference building for the simulations. The energy reference area is 1205 m2 and consists of common areas and three residential floors with a total of six dwellings. The zones and internal loads correspond to the specifications of SIA 2024 [49]. The building envelope was designed in a way that the heating requirement meets the Swiss Minergie standard for sustainable buildings, with a standard space heating demand of 27.1 kWh/(m2 a) for the reference weather station Zurich SMA. The results obtained with a detailed building model from IDA-ICE were used to fit a simplified building model in TRNSYS that is based on ISO 13790 standard [52].

Household electricity (HHEl) and Domestic Hot Water (DHW) demand profiles were created using the Load Profile Generator software [53]. A single profile for each of the six dwellings was considered:

•

Couple under 30 years old, both at work;

•

family with two children (14 and 16), one at work, one at home;

•

family both at work, two children (9 and 12);

•

retired couple, both at home;

•

shift worker couple;

•

family, two children (6 and 12), both parents at home.

The six single profiles for HHEl and DHW of the apartments were merged to one HHEl and one DHW profile. In order to check whether the profiles are realistic, the total annual consumption for each household was compared separately with values from the literature as presented by Nipkow et al. [54] and the SIA 2024 standard guidelines [49].

The DHW profile shows an annual tapped consumption of 16.6 MWh. The DHW demand is in the order of 15.5 kWh/m2 of heated surface area. Circulation losses were included such that the minimum return temperature of the circulation loop was 53 °C for legionella safety. The pipe length from the circulation loop has been defined such that the circulation losses were around 30% of the DHW demand. In total, the DHW demand, including circulation losses, accounted for 20 kWh per m2 heated surface. An overview of the electricity and heat loads is given in Table 3.

Table 3. Specific and yearly demands for space heating, domestic hot water and electricity for the multi-family building in Zurich.

kWh/m2MWh

Domestic hot water (without/with circulation)15.5/20.216.6

2.5. Key performance indicators

In order to assess the performance of the proposed system, several Key Performance Indicators (KPI) need to be defined. The most important KPI on the energetic side is the PV generation ratio RPV,gen=ElPV/Elused+Elsmelter, where ElPV is the electricity generated by the PV field, Elused is the electricity consumed in the building including all losses and Elsmelter is the electricity used to regenerate the aluminium in the Al-smelter.

Ensuring that enough energy is generated is not sufficient. It is necessary that the energy can be used in the system when necessary without the need of purchasing electricity from the grid. In order to quantify these effects, the ratio of self-sufficiency Rself,suff=Elused-Elgrid/Elused, where Elgrid is the electricity purchased from the grid.

The economic KPI to assess the affordability of the proposed system is based on present value of energy cost in €/kWh, calculated using the annuity method described in VDI 2607 [57].

2.6. Life cycle assessment (LCA)

At this early stage of investigation of a novel process, only a limited number of impact factors were analysed in the LCA. These are:

•

CED total: Cumulated total primary Energy Demand

•

CED non-renewable: Cumulated non-renewable primary Energy Demand

•

IPCC 2013 GWP100a: Global Warming Potential for 100 years

The proposed system concept for heat and electricity supply of a multi-family building is compared with a conventional gas boiler for heating and DHW in combination with electricity consumption from the ENTSO-E low voltage grid. The LCA data was extracted from SimaPro 8.3.0.0, with inventory data from the Ecoinvent database version 3.3, using the “cut-off” system model. Where the size of the component or amount of a service or product in the database did not match the size of the component of our system, data was linearly extrapolated. The impact of the Al-to-H2 converter is assumed to be negligible. For the aluminium production from aluminium hydroxide, the electricity is provided by the PV plant, and therefore the impact of electric energy use in aluminium production had to be subtracted from the corresponding Ecoinvent dataset, in order not to count it twice. The same applies for the alumina input that is not obtained from bauxite mining but from the aluminium converter. It was assumed that bauxite has only to be mined once and then the energy cycle can be run for at least 30 years with the alumina obtained, i.e. the impact of bauxite mining was counted with a factor of 0.033.

2.7. Cost estimations

Although it is possible that first systems for seasonal energy storage with aluminium may run as early as 2022, a large scale application is more likely from the year 2030 onward. Thus, our cost estimations are made for the year 2030, including cost decrease compared to today, mainly for components that have only recently been introduced into the markets and are not applied at large scale yet.

2.7.1. Capex

2.7.1.1. Photovoltaics

Rooftop photovoltaic systems are state of the art today and cost roughly 1500 €/kWp today. A study of the EU PV Platform [58] predicts a cost of 900 €/kWp for 5 kWp, about 700 €/kWp for 50 kWp and 530 €/kWp for 1 MWp in 2030. We assume a base cost of 1000 € plus 700 €/kWp for 2030.

2.7.1.2. Fuel cell system

According to [46], more than 140,000 micro-CHP fuel cells have been sold and installed in Japan up to now. Panasonic launched an Ene-Farm fuel cell of 0.7 kWel system in Japan promising a durability of 70,000 h, which is sold for around $12,700 before tax and installation. In general, fuel cells for electricity supply of buildings are still an emerging technology and cost roughly 10,000–20,000 €/kWel today [45]. Some forecasts predict future prices below 1000 €/kWel once economy of scale will have its impact [59]. In stationary applications for buildings, hydrogen for the fuel cells is often obtained from reforming of natural gas from gas grids since this energy carrier is readily available, whereas hydrogen is difficult to store and transport. When hydrogen is obtained from aluminium oxidation, the methane or natural gas reformation process can be spared, which reduces energetic losses as well as cost for the fuel cell system. For this study we assumed a base cost of 1000 € plus 1000 €/kWel for a system without methane reforming in the year 2030.

2.7.1.3. Battery system

Although the cost of a home battery system is today still in the range of 1500 €/kWh capacity [60], battery packs for automotive application are already available at prices of roughly 500 €/kWh, and this cost is expected to be further reduced to 170 €/kWh in 2025 [61]. We assume a system cost of 450 € + 230 €/kWh for a home battery system >10 kWh in 2030.

2.7.1.4. Heat pumps

Patteeuw et al. [62] estimated the cost of an air source heat pump for low temperature heat distribution with 7650 € base cost plus 410 €/kW. Based on our experience from Switzerland we assume a base cost of 6000 € plus 450 €/kWp. For a 26 kW heat pump, our investment cost is thus 7.6% higher than the cost reported in literature.

2.7.1.5. Thermal storage

We assume a base cost of 700 € plus 700 €/m3, which is roughly what thermal storages cost today (e.g. Universal Energy Storage as offered in [63]).

2.7.1.6. Al-to-H2 converter

At this moment it is difficult to estimate the cost of a device that has never been built. However, we currently estimate that an aluminium-to-H2 converter may be offered with a base cost of 1500 € plus 1300 €/(kg/h).

2.7.2. Annuity cost

The annuity cost of the CAPEX has been calculated with the OPEX, the different lifetime of the components, and an interest rate of 3%.

2.7.3. OPEX

The operational expenditures include 1% of the investment cost of all CAPEX per year. Additionally, transporting electricity from the private PV rooftop installations to the smelter plant is reimbursed with 0.05 €/kWh. This corresponds roughly to fees for grid services for private consumption >25,000 kWh/a that were lower than 0.056 €/kWh for most cantons of Switzerland in 2019 [64]. Additionally, the energy service company (ESCO) that is running the aluminium smelter plant and organizing the transport of the aluminium fuel to the site of use as well as the transport back is reimbursed with 1.2 € per kg of Al. Literature [65] suggests that aluminium can be produced at a cost of 150–300 US$/ton with non-carbon electrode technology. This price does not include electric energy purchase, which in our case is included in the cost of PV electricity and grid service; and it does not account for alumina raw material purchase, which in our case is recycled from the distributed units that produce electricity and heat from aluminium. It is assumed that our smelter plant only runs for roughly one third of the year, when renewable energy is abundant, and therefore the “normal” production cost of roughly 200 €/ton is doubled to 400 €/ton. Subtracting these 400 €/ton from the 1200 €/ton that the ESCO receives results in 800 €/ton for transport cost and margins.

3. Results

3.1. Energy balances

Energy balances are calculated for all individual components, hydraulic loops and also for the entire system. The energy imbalances were always below 1% with respect to the total energy demand.

In order to illustrate how the system performs, simulation results for Zurich that achieve RPv,gen and Rself-suff above 99% are presented hereafter.

The monthly and yearly energy balances of the system are shown in Fig. 7. On the left plot of Fig. 7 the overall energy balance, including heat and electricity, is shown with the following energy quantities:

•

Heat from air: is the renewable energy extracted from the air by the evaporator of the air source heat pump.

•

Heat from Al-to-H2 and fuel cell: is the heat released by the chemical reaction of the Al to obtain H2 from the reaction shown in Eq. (5) and the heat released by the fuel cell in order to convert the H2 into electricity. This heat is transferred to the thermal storage.

•

PV to system: is the total net AC electricity provided by the PV system (field and inverters).

•

El from grid: is the total electricity purchased from the grid. We set the goal to limit this flow to <2% of total electricity use.

•

El from fuel cell: is the electricity provide by the fuel cell

•

DHW: is the DHW demand

•

SH: is the space heating demand

•

HH El: is the household electricity demand

•

Loss heat and El: are the heat losses of the storage tank, pipes and the electrical losses of the battery.

From the left plot of Fig. 7 it can be observed that most of the energy supply in winter is needed to cover the heat demands (DHW and SH) by the heat pump and by heat from the Al-to-H2 converter and the fuel cell. The heat pump uses ambient air as low temperature heat source and is driven by electricity provided by the PV field and the fuel cell. The remaining electricity production that is provided by these two elements is used to cover the household electricity of the building.

The yearly shares of the energy inputs that cover all the energy needs of the building and all the losses are shown on the right plot of Fig. 7. The ambient air used by the heat pump accounts for 36% of the total share. The compressor of the heat pump needs around 12% of the total energy input and the heat pump will provide 48% of the total energy in the form of heat. The other sources of heat are the Al-to-H2 converter and the fuel cell, which together provide 22% of the energy needs as heat, and 7% of the energy needs as electricity. With the assumptions of Table 4 this leads to a demand of 2662 kg of aluminium (443 kg per apartment). The PV field covers 34% of the electricity demand directly or via the local battery, and only 1% is purchased from the grid. The electricity flows of the system are shown on the left plot of Fig. 8. Most of the electricity demand is used for covering the household electricity (HH El) and a smaller part is used for the heating system (El used by heat system), which is basically the heat pump compressor but it also includes circulation pumps and controls. The PV production is distributed into direct use for household electricity (PV to HH El) and heating system (PV to heat system) and indirect use by means of the battery. The indirect use of PV is included into the electricity provided by the battery (El from battery) which accounts for about 50% of the electricity provided to the building.

In order to regenerate the Al it is necessary that the excess PV that is fed to the grid is enough to cover the electricity demand of the calcination step and smelter plant that produces the same amount of Al as is used by the Al-to-H2 converter. The monthly and yearly distribution of the PV production is shown on the right plot of Fig. 8. The first priority of the control is to provide the building with electricity. Roughly half of the building needs are provided directly by the PV and the other half are provided through the battery. However, even in some winter months, most of the energy provided by PV is used to regenerate the Al in the smelter plant. This is surprising and might indicate room for improvement of the dimensioning and control of the components.

Most likely, the Al-smelter plant will not be operated in winter. All excess electricity in winter is likely to be always welcome by grid operators since it is the time where electricity will be scarce.

3.2. Results on different locations

3.2.1. Energy balances

Four locations as described in Section 2.4.1 have been considered in order to assess the system concept in different climatic regions. Results of the PV generation ratio RPV,gen are shown on Fig. 9 for the different locations and for two battery sizes as a function of the size of the PV system (PVpeak). The results that are of interest are those in which the RPV,gen is 100%. The influence of the battery is significantly depending on the location. Interestingly, results for Davos are influenced much more by the battery size than results for Zurich.

The space heating demand for each location is shown in Table 6. Locarno is the location where the space heat demand is lowest and 100% RPVgen can be achieved with around 39 kWp of PV installation. Davos has the highest heating demand. However, compared to Zurich and Geneva it needs significantly lower PV capacity to achieve the target of 100% RPVgen. Geneva needs also significantly lower installed PV capacity compared to Zurich, while the heat demand is only slightly lower. The main reason for these large differences are the availability of solar radiation and its distribution over the year.

Table 6. Specific and yearly demands for space heating depending on the location.

LocationSpecific SH demanda)Total SH demand
kWh/m2MWh
Davos42.042.9
Zurich27.129.1
Geneva24.222.6
Locarno16.617.8
a)

Demand per heated floor surface area.

A comparison of the energy shares provided to the system (positive) and extracted/delivered by the system (negative) are shown in Fig. 10 for Davos and Zurich. Even that the installed PV capacity is 30% lower in Davos, the share that PV contributes directly is slightly higher compared to Zurich. In Davos, much less energy is provided by the Al-to-H2 converter, which means that less Al is needed for the Al redox cycle. The lower amount of energy provided by the Al-to-H2 converter is compensated by ambient air in the heat pump, i.e. by direct heat pump operation with PV electricity.

3.2.2. Cost analysis

The energy cost of the systems as a function of the PV generation ratio RPVgen are shown in Fig. 11. Results for RPVgen = 100% are detailed in Table 7.

Table 7. Cost and installed PV capacity that reach a RPVgen > 99% and Rself,suff > 98% with a battery of 100 kWh.

LocationCapacity of PV systemCostAmount of Al
kWp (m2)a)€/kWhkg
Davos42.3 (252 m2)0.142198
Zurich60.5 (360 m2)0.202662
Geneva48.7 (290 m2)0.202740
Locarno39 (232 m2)0.191858
a)

Area is calculated with a nominal power of 186 W/m2. Lower areas are needed for modules with higher efficiency.

The energy cost of Davos are in the range of 0.14 €/kWh and thus well below the cost of all the other locations. It is worth to notice that simulations in Davos are neglecting the possibility of snow covering the PV field and therefore the cost may be underestimated. The cost of the other locations vary between 0.19 €/kWh and 0.20 €/kWh. Among these, Locarno shows the lowest specific cost, followed by Geneva and finally by Zurich.

Details of the investment cost and energy generation cost are provided in Table 8 for the city of Zurich. The cost assumptions have been provide in Section 2.8. The investment cost shares and annuity cost for energy generation are shown in Fig. 12. According to this evaluation and the assumed cost, the most relevant investment cost are the PV installation and the battery. Regarding the annuity shares, 52% of the cost are allocated to the investment cost, followed by the service of the ESCO that is regenerating aluminium as fuel (24%) by running the power-to-X process and the cost for using the electricity grid (15%) to transmit the excess electricity from the generation to the Al-smelter

Table 8. Investment cost and energy generation cost using a 100 kWh battery for Zurich. Interest rate for annuity calculation: 3%.

Major ComponentCosts [€]SizeLife time [years]Total Costs [€]
PV1000 + 700/kWp60 kWp2543,350
Battery450 + 230/kWh100 kWh1523,450
Thermal Storage700 + 700/m33.5 m3403150
Heat pump6000 + 450/kW26 kW2517′700
Fuel Cell1000 + 1000/kWel10 kWel2011′000
Al reactor1500 + 1300/kg/h5 kg/h258′000
Total Investment Cost106,650
Annuity costyearly costs over lifetime13,131/a
Investment6805/a
Electricity from grid (0.3 €/kWh)289 kWh87/a
Maintenance (1% of investment cost)1066/a
Aluminium fuel service (1.2 €/kg)2662 kg3195/a
Transmission grid (0.05 €/kWh)39,934 kWh1997/a
Energy generation costsBased on annuity cost0.20€/kWh

3.3. Results of environmental life cycle assessment

An overview of the results from the LCA is given in Table 9 for the 100% solar system with the aluminium seasonal energy storage, and in Table 10 for a reference system with natural gas heating and electricity from the grid. Fig. 13 shows impressively that for the conventional system only the gas and electricity consumption are relevant. For the 100% solar PV driven system with Al seasonal storage, the PV system is by far the most dominant factor if inert electrode technology is used for the aluminium production. The conventional Hall-Héroult smelter process would increase the non-renewable primary energy demand by less than 20%, but it would increase the GWP by 52%. However, even with the conventional smelter process the PV system remains the dominant impact factor for both, the non-renewable primary energy demand as well as the GWP. Transport of aluminium fuel from the industrial site to the point of use and transport of Al(OH)3 back to the industrial site has been taken into account with a distance of 100 km each. Since roughly 3 metric tons of Al(OH)3 have to be transported back for each metric ton of Al that is used, this leads to 1065 t-km for the transport service. The LCA data-set for a 7.5–15 metric ton lorry of emission category Euro 6 has been used for the calculation. It shows that the impact of transport is almost negligible as long as the distance remains shorter than 100 km, but may become quite significant for distances in the range of 1000 km or more.

Table 9. LCA data for the assessment of the 100% solar heat and electricity system.

ComponentSize/amountLifetime (years)CED total (kWh oil eq/a)CED non-renewable (kWh oil eq/a)GWP 100a (kg CO2 eq/a)
Air-water heat pump26 kW25370341276
PV system60 kWp2526′11222′5366′547
Li-ion battery100 kWh155′9875′3711′485
Fuel cell10 kWel202′5312′306719
TES3500 l40449387117
Transport of Al and Al(OH)31065 tkm110151001225
Aluminium smeltinga)2662 kg146724485370
Total41,13536,4289739
Additional impact of Carbon anodes4′8364′6614′929
a)

with inert anodes and assuming Al replacement every 30 years (3.3% loss rate).

Table 10. LCA data for a conventional gas boiler and electricity from the ENTSO-E grid.

ComponentSize/amountLifetime (years)CED total (kWh oil eq)CED non-renewable (kWh oil eq)GWP 100a (kg CO2 eq)
Useful heat from gas boiler45,640 kWh155,37854,87111,896
Electricity*19,703 kWh162,30854,0129840
TES1200 l2524621264
Total117,932109,09521,801
*

from grid ENTSO-E low voltage.

Overall, the new system concept with inert electrode technology is estimated to reduce non-renewable primary energy consumption by a factor of three, while it reduces GWP by 55%.

While for the conventional system the emissions are inherently connected to the consumption of natural gas and electricity from the grid, in the 100% solar system the emissions are connected to the embodied energy in the PV installation and thus reflect the share of renewable energy used for the production of the PV panels, the mounting system and inverters. This opens the possibility to decrease the GWP and non-renewable primary energy consumption by increasing the efficiency of PV modules or by increasing the share of renewable energies in the PV production process.

4. Conclusions

Long term energy storage will be essential for the energy transition, especially for countries with a high heat demand in winter, but limited potential to increase renewable energy generation or import renewable energy in this season at affordable cost.

4.1. Energy storage capacity of aluminium

Aluminium has a high storage density. Theoretically, 8.7 kWh of heat and electricity can be produced from 1 kg of Al, which is in the range of heating oil, and on a volumetric base (23.5 MWh/m3) even surpasses the energy density of heating oil by a factor of two.

4.2. The Power-to-Al process

Aluminium is produced in large quantities worldwide from aluminium oxide (alumina) with the Hall-Héroult process with roughly 50% energetic efficiency (electric and carbon-anode input to chemical energy stored in Al). This electrolysis process uses carbon anodes that are consumed and converted to CO2 by the redox reaction. Since these carbon anodes are usually made from coal and tar of fossil origin, this process emits substantial amounts of greenhouse gases. Therefore, inert or non-consumable electrode processes should be used for an Al redox energy storage cycle to reduce greenhouse gas emissions as much as possible. Literature suggests that the process of inert electrode Al smelting could reach an electric efficiency higher than 50%, i.e. 60% or even 65%.

4.3. Production of heat and hydrogen from aluminium

The production of heat and hydrogen from aluminium with subsequent conversion of hydrogen in a fuel cell to electricity, heat and water may be carried out at temperatures of 50–70 °C. Ideally, this yields roughly 6.5 kWh of heat and 2.2 kWh of electricity, assuming a complete reaction of Al to aluminium hydroxide and an electric efficiency of the fuel cell of 50%. It is expected that the components necessary for these conversions can be built with moderate cost and require little maintenance, such that they can be installed and operated in single or multi-family houses. However, the conversion of alumina to aluminium requires a smelting process that involves a highly aggressive electrolyte and takes place at 750–960 °C. This process is unlikely to be suitable for an application in a residential building. Therefore, it is proposed that the envisaged energy storage cycle will be split into the “storage charging” reactions that consist in converting aluminium hydroxide to alumina and then to aluminium, and the “storage discharging” reactions that produce heat and electricity. While the charging reactions take place at an industrial site and with larger scale conversion units, the discharging is done where and when heat is needed and can be carried out in small buildings down to the size of single- and multi-family homes. In this energy storage system, aluminium “fuel” is transported from the industrial site to the individual buildings and aluminium hydroxide is transported back to the industrial site.

4.4. 100% solar energy supply for buildings

It is proposed that owners of individual buildings extend their solar and PV heat and electricity supply systems with an aluminium conversion unit and become prosumers in the sense that they produce and export electricity in summer for conversion into an aluminium “fuel” at the industrial site, and consume this Al in winter. This requires new business models and contracts between the home owners and energy service companies (ESCOs) that transport electricity and fuel and manage the aluminium hydroxide calcination and aluminium smelting facilities.

Our simulations of a six dwelling multi-family house insulated according to the current legal requirements in different climates of Switzerland show that 1800–2800 kg of Al “fuel” would have to be used for the supply of heat and electricity in winter in combination with a PV plant size of 39–61 kWp, a heat pump and an electric storage. This corresponds to 300 to 470 kg Al and 6.5 – 10.2 kWp PV per dwelling. Our cost estimation for the year 2030, that includes anticipated cost reductions compared to today, shows that energy prices (heat and electricity mixed) in the range of 0.20 €/kWh (end-consumer, CAPEX + OPEX) may be realistic. Thus, 100% solar heating and electricity supply of buildings may be possible at a price that is competitive to today's energy prices in some places and for some of the traditional energy carriers, if CAPEX of traditional converters (burners, storage) and installations (grid connections, exhaust ducts or chimneys) are accounted for.

In our simulations and cost calculations, short term battery storage has been included at the building level, but it is well possible that better economics can be achieved by installing batteries at district level by the ESCOs to provide the short term flexibility as a service to the home owners.

4.5. Environmental LCA

In terms of primary energy use and greenhouse gas emissions, our analysis shows that non-renewable primary energy consumption can be reduced by 62% and greenhouse gas emissions can be reduced by 33% compared to a conventional system with natural gas heating and electricity from the ENTSO-E low voltage grid if today's Hall-Héroult process is used for aluminium smelting. If carbon anodes can be replaced by inert or non-consumable anodes that do not lead to CO2 and other greenhouse gas emissions from the smelting process, then the reductions are in the range of 67% for non-renewable primary energy and 55% for GWP100. The main contributor to non-renewable energy consumption and GWP100 is the production and installation of the PV plant. It is expected that this impact will be reduced in the future, as a consequence of higher PV module efficiencies and progress in PV manufacturing technologies, and due to a higher share of renewable energy supply in the production of PV panels and mounting materials.

Declaration of Competing Interest

The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

Acknowledgements

This research was supported by the Swiss Federal Office of Energy (SFOE) in the frame of the project HybridStock (SI/501693-01) and by HSR University of Applied Sciences of Rapperswil, Switzerland.

Symbols
